nativeRange.habitat: Sandy shoreline shell beds in Lake Tanganyika; almost always found in colonies inhabiting dense accumulations of empty Neothauma tanganyicense snail shells over soft sandy substrate

decorPreferences: abundant empty snail shells (at least 3 per fish; Neothauma or similar 5 cm opening shells preferred)
fine sand substrate 2–5 cm deep across most of the tank floor
open sandy areas for territory display and foraging
sparse hardy plants (Java fern, Anubias, floating plants) as shelldwellers uproot rooted plants
minimal rockwork or driftwood to break sightlines between competing males

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore feeding primarily on small invertebrates. Offer varied live and frozen foods: baby brine shrimp, microworms, daphnia, cyclops, and finely crushed frozen bloodworms. High-quality nano pellets and crushed flakes accepted but should not dominate the diet. Wild fish consume copepods, ostracods, and insect larvae. Feed small amounts once or twice daily; avoid dry-food-only diets as variety and protein quality are critical to long-term health and coloration.

goodWith: Neolamprologus brichardi and other small rockdwelling lamprologines
Cyprichromis species (open-water schoolers, occupy upper column)
other Lake Tanganyika species that occupy different zones
avoidWith: other shell-dwelling Neolamprologus species (compete for shells and territory)
larger aggressive cichlids that would harass or consume them
fast or boisterous tankmates that disrupt colony structure
any fish small enough to be harassed out of shell territories

imageCandidates.notes: Species reference image from FishBase summary page for Neolamprologus similis.

summary: Neolamprologus similis is a diminutive Lake Tanganyika shell dweller closely related to N. multifasciatus, distinguished by iridescent zebra stripes extending all the way to the eyes. Despite reaching only 3.5–5 cm, colonies defend Neothauma shell territories with outsized aggression. Intermediate care due to strict hard alkaline water and shell-habitat requirements.
careNotes:
The Similis Shell Dweller is one of the smallest cichlids in the hobby, endemic to the sandy shell-bed zones of Lake Tanganyika along the shores of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Tanzania. It is often confused with its close relative N. multifasciatus (“multis”); the key visual distinction is that similis’ horizontal stripes extend all the way to the eyes, while multis’ stripes stop behind the gill plate. Some taxonomic debate exists over whether the two are truly separate species or represent variants of a single species, though most current authorities treat them as distinct.

The most important aspect of husbandry is providing an abundance of appropriately sized empty snail shells — ideally Neothauma tanganyicense or equivalent shells with approximately 5 cm openings. Provide at least three shells per fish; more is always better. Spread shells across a fine sand substrate over most of the tank floor, leaving open sandy corridors for territory display. This species is not a strong digger and prefers a shallower substrate layer than many other shell dwellers.

Water chemistry must mirror Lake Tanganyika: hard (gH 8–25), alkaline (pH 7.5–9.0), and warm (24–27°C). Soft or acidic water rapidly leads to health decline. Crushed coral or aragonite sand helps maintain buffering capacity and alkalinity. Regular partial water changes (20–25% weekly) are essential.

For colony keeping, a group of 6 or more is recommended to establish natural social dynamics, with a ratio of 2–3 females per male. Males establish and defend small territories (typically 15 cm across) centered on their shell cluster and will vigorously challenge intruders, including human hands. Despite their territorial nature, similis can be housed successfully with non-competing Lake Tanganyika species that occupy rock or open-water zones. Do not mix with other shell-dwelling species, especially N. multifasciatus, as competition is intense and hybridization may occur.

See also: [many-banded-shell-dweller] (N. multifasciatus) — sister species; [neolamprologus-brichardi] — compatible rock-dwelling lamprologine.
breedingNotes: Shell brooders that breed readily in a well-set-up colony tank. Males maintain harems of 2–5 females, each female selecting and defending a single shell as her spawning site. Females deposit small clutches of eggs inside the shell interior; the male fertilizes them externally and then guards the shell cluster while the female tends the eggs directly. Eggs hatch within approximately 24 hours; fry become free-swimming around day 6–7 and initially remain close to the shell entrance under maternal protection. Fry are evicted from the female’s shell after roughly 2 weeks but are often tolerated within the broader colony territory, particularly females. Male fry are eventually expelled as they mature and become territorial rivals. First foods: baby brine shrimp nauplii, microworms, or finely powdered fry food. Breeding difficulty is moderate — the colony breeds without special intervention once conditions are stable, but raising fry alongside adults requires adequate shell density and tank size to prevent predation by the male or neighboring females.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Neolamprologus similis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males 4.5 cm, females 3.5 cm), water parameters (temp 24–27°C, pH 7.5–9.0, hardness 8–25°H), minimum tank dimensions (45×30×30 cm / 40 L for a pair), habitat requirements (shell beds over fine sand), tankmate recommendations, breeding notes (shell brooder, eggs hatch ~24 h, fry free-swimming day 6–7), and behavior description.

additional.name: FishBase — Neolamprologus similis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 5.0 cm TL), temperature range (23–28°C), pH (7.4–8.4), hardness (dH 7–30), depth range (10–45 m), native range (DRC and Tanzania), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and diet (copepods, ostracods, insect larvae). Described by Büscher 1992.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Shell Dwellers
additional.notes: Confirmed visual distinction from N. multifasciatus (stripes reach eyes in similis), tank size recommendations (10–20 gal), water parameters (pH 7.5–9.0, temp 75–80°F, gH ≥8°), minimum 3 shells per fish, diet (carnivorous: brine shrimp, microworms, nano pellets, crushed flakes), and colony group size (start with 6+).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Neolamprologus similis
additional.notes: Confirmed endemic range (DRC and Tanzania), IUCN Least Concern status, former synonym Lamprologus similis, colony/harem social structure, and sex-biased fry tolerance behavior (female fry tolerated and eventually courted; male fry expelled).

additional.name: AquAnswers — Similis Cichlid (Neolamprologus similis)
additional.notes: Supplementary data on lifespan (8–10 years in stable conditions), harem breeding structure (1 male, 3–5 females), feeding frequency guidance, and additional tankmate and incompatibility notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Shell brooder; breeds readily in colony tanks with adequate shells and stable Tanganyikan water chemistry. Females lay eggs inside shells; male guards the cluster. Fry free-swimming day 6–7; accept baby brine shrimp nauplii and microworms. Male fry eventually expelled; female fry may be retained in the colony.
